The word platypus comes from Greek platys (flat) and pous (foot).
Male platypuses have a spur on their hind foot that delivers a potent venom.
When scientists first saw a specimen, they thought it was a hoax made of different animals sewn together.
Platypuses are one of only five species of mammals that lay eggs.
Male platypuses have venomous spurs on their hind legs.
Platypuses can detect electric fields generated by their prey using their bills.
A baby platypus is called a puggle.
Platypuses don't have stomachs. Their esophagus connects directly to their intestines.
Platypuses close their eyes and ears while swimming and rely on their bill to navigate.
The platypus is the animal emblem of New South Wales, Australia.
Platypuses can store up to 900 insects in their cheek pouches.
A platypus can consume its own body weight in food in a 24 hour period.
Platypuses have two layers of fur that trap air next to their skin, keeping them dry and buoyant for the 12 hours they spend underwater each day searching for food.
